era_context: Contemporaneous with the previous year: Middle Kingdom Egypt under Amenemhat III; mature Indus Valley Civilization; early Shang or late Xia in China (dating disputed); Minoan Crete in the Middle Minoan II period. This was an era of regional consolidation, long-distance trade, and the development of complex administrative systems across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (limited written records; archaeological context available but not year-specific)

events.title: Continued reign of Amenemhat III in Egypt
events.description: Amenemhat III's reign continued with ongoing administrative stability, building projects, and control of trade networks. This year falls within his long reign (c. 1860–1814 BCE in conventional chronology). No specific events are recorded for this particular year in surviving sources; the year is significant only as part of the broader context of Middle Kingdom stability.
events.certainty_note: No specific events are attested for 2011 BCE. The year is assigned to Amenemhat III's reign based on conventional chronology, with the same ±50-year uncertainty as the previous year.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization trade networks active
events.region: Indus Valley and broader trade networks
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization maintained extensive trade networks with Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia. Seals, weights, and artifacts from Indus sites have been found in Mesopotamian contexts, and vice versa. This trade was crucial to the Indus economy and to the distribution of luxury goods across the ancient world. No specific events are recorded for this year; the significance is in the ongoing economic integration of the Indus Valley into broader Eurasian trade systems.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Indus sites and Mesopotamian sites

events.certainty_note: Trade networks are well-attested archaeologically for the mature Indus phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE). No specific events are recorded for 2011 BCE; the year is assigned based on the broader chronological context.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are attested for 2011 BCE in any tradition or archaeological record. The year is assigned to broader historical contexts (Amenemhat III's reign, the mature Indus phase) based on conventional chronology, which itself is disputed. No disconfirming evidence is identified because no specific claims are made for this year.
historiographic_note: 2011 BCE is not a year for which any specific historical events are recorded in surviving sources. The historical record for this year is essentially empty; what is known is only the broader context of the civilizations active at this time. This reflects the general sparseness of year-specific historical documentation for the early 2nd millennium BCE. The year is included in this chronicle for completeness, but it illustrates the limits of historical knowledge for this period.
